preFix.title: Watercolour Paper Properties
preFix.subtitle: Sizing, surface, cotton content, and weight in watercolour papers
preFix.excerpt: Not all watercolour paper behaves the same way with the same paint. The differences are structural: internal sizing versus surface sizing, cold-press versus hot-press surface, the proportion of cotton in the sheet, and the weight in gsm all change how the paper accepts water, holds pigment, and behaves when wet. Understanding these properties helps in choosing paper and in knowing how to prepare handmade sheets for painting.
preFix.sourceNotes: Hunter, Dard. Old Papermaking. Chillicothe, Ohio: Mountain House Press, 1923. Sections on sizing and surface treatment; supplemented by general knowledge of paper manufacture.

content.text: The two types of sizing

Sizing controls how water moves through or sits on paper. Unsized paper is called waterleaf: it absorbs liquid immediately and entirely, spreading it in all directions. No watercolour work is possible on waterleaf paper because pigment blooms and cannot be controlled.
The two sizing approaches produce different behaviour.
Internal sizing

(also called engine sizing) coats every fibre in the sheet. Water that hits the surface is not repelled but slowed: it can still soak through given time and pressure, but does not do so at once. This gives the painter time to work wet-on-wet before the paper is saturated.

Internal sizing also affects the dry-back behaviour of paint: as the sheet dries, the internal size holds pigment approximately where it was placed. On a weakly internally sized paper, paint creeps as the water finds pathways through less-sized areas.
Surface sizing

with gelatin creates a harder top layer. It improves the paper's resistance to lifting and scrubbing, allows more aggressive rework without fibre damage, and produces a harder, crisper edge to wet-into-wet blooms. Most high-quality watercolour papers are both internally and surface-sized.

Student-grade papers are usually internally sized only. The surface is softer, lifts more easily when wet, and the pigment sink is faster. These are valid choices for practice but make certain techniques (granulating washes, wet-into-wet with large amounts of water) more difficult to control.
Cold-press and hot-press surfaces
The
cold-press

surface is the standard for most watercolour work. The press does not apply heat; the slight texture of the press felt transfers to the paper surface, creating microscopic peaks and valleys. This tooth holds pigment and gives washes their characteristic luminosity, because light entering between pigment particles reflects back through the paper. Cold-press is sold as NOT in many ranges, meaning not hot-pressed.

Hot-press paper is pressed between heated rollers, which collapses the surface texture into a smooth, hard plane. Pigment sits on rather than in the surface. The result is excellent for fine-line work, detailed botanical illustration, and pen-and-wash because the smooth surface accepts both drawing instruments and fluid paint without the roughness catching a nib. Large flat washes are harder on hot-press because they dry with a slight unevenness: the paint finds nowhere to hide.
Rough watercolour paper is the third surface category: cold-press but with a more pronounced felt texture. It is not discussed in detail here but the same sizing principles apply.
Cotton content and water retention
The paper industry produces watercolour papers from two main fibre sources: cotton and wood pulp. The distinction matters enormously in use.

Cotton fibre has a natural twist (convolution) along its length. When wet, this twist allows the fibre to absorb a large amount of water relative to its mass without losing structural integrity. A 100% cotton sheet can be thoroughly wetted, crumpled, and dried flat repeatedly without the fibre breaking down. This resilience is essential for watercolour technique that involves multiple wet passes.

Cotton also dries more slowly than wood pulp paper. The higher water retention means that a wet-into-wet bloom started on cotton has more working time before the paper surface shifts from damp to dry. This is not simply because the paper is thicker; 300 gsm wood pulp paper dries faster than 300 gsm cotton paper because the cotton fibres hold water more tenaciously.

Wood pulp paper is cheaper and perfectly adequate for practice. Its fibres are more uniform in length but weaker when wet. Vigorous scrubbing lifts fibres to the surface, creating a texture that subsequent washes sit on unevenly. Repeated wetting weakens the sheet structure progressively; the paper can pill or develop bald patches.
Weight and buckling
Watercolour papers are sold by weight in gsm (grams per square metre). The most common grades are 190 gsm, 300 gsm, and 640 gsm. Weight is the primary factor in whether a sheet will buckle when wet.

190 gsm paper will buckle visibly with any significant water. It is usable for small, relatively dry work or when mounted to a board before painting. 300 gsm is the standard professional choice: it will buckle slightly at the edges when heavily wetted but not enough to cause pooling. 640 gsm is heavy enough that it can be wetted without buckling at all, but it is expensive and heavy for large formats.

The traditional solution for 190 gsm paper is stretching: the sheet is soaked, then stapled or taped to a wooden board while wet. As it dries, it shrinks flat against the board. When wet again during painting, the tension holds it flat. Gummed brown tape adhered to the wet paper on all four edges is the simplest approach.
Implications for handmade watercolour paper

A handmade sheet made from 100% cotton fibre is close to a good commercial watercolour paper in structure, but the prep steps must be done carefully. An unsized cotton sheet, no matter how well formed, cannot hold a watercolour wash. You must surface-size it with dilute gelatin.

Gelatin sizing on a dry, restraint-dried cotton sheet gives a surface that takes watercolour much like a cold-press commercial paper. The result depends on how strong the gelatin is. More gelatin gives a harder surface that takes longer to re-wet. Less gelatin gives a softer surface that absorbs paint a little faster.

A starting proportion for gelatin sizing is 1 to 2 g of leaf gelatin per 100 ml of warm water. Brush this onto the dry sheet and allow to dry flat. A second coat, applied after the first is fully dry, produces a surface closer to double-sized commercial paper.

Weight of the handmade sheet is controlled by pulp consistency and number of passes over the mould. Thicker sheets take more forming time but produce the 300 gsm equivalent that resists buckling. Testing gsm is straightforward: cut a 10 cm square from a dry sized sheet and weigh it. Multiply the weight in grams by 100 to get gsm.
